message.content:
Task id: T70 (ledgerRefs goals:G5)
Base commit: 817dac4 (main) — your worktree may be cut from an older HEAD; FIRST run `git reset --hard 817dac4` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T70` so your commit lands on the task branch.

NOTE: Go repository (wanbond). Gate: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` (no bun). Fetch the FULL task text from the ledger (fetch_item tasks T70) — it carries the authoritative revised description with the R70 spec-scoped-identity requirements. Summary of the core requirements:

headline: Refactor hubFailover to a mutable, spec-keyed endpoint set updated under its lock

Key points (full text in the ledger): replace hubFailover's immutable endpoints []netip.AddrPort snapshot (internal/device/failover.go) with an ordered []config.EndpointSpec structure where each spec owns its current expansion ([]netip.AddrPort); flattened concatenation is the failover order; ACTIVE endpoint tracked by (specIdx, AddrPort) — never a raw flattened index or bare value (cross-spec duplicate AddrPorts are legal at runtime); updateResolution(specIdx, addrs) under h.mu swaps a spec's expansion — active entry stays if its AddrPort survives within its owning spec (re-map idx), repoint via ONE SetPeerRemote + rehandshake only when the active entry's AddrPort changed (D32 no-op suppression), standby-only changes never touch the bond; check() keeps its exact semantics over the flattened list; startHubFailover constructs a controller when the peer has ANY hostname spec OR >=2 flattened endpoints (single-IP-literal peer: NO controller, byte-for-byte pre-G5); do NOT wire the resolver here.

acceptance: go test ./internal/device/... -race passes: all existing failover_test.go tests unchanged; new fake-clock/fake-health/fake-remote cases prove (1) standby-record swap → zero SetPeerRemote; (2) active-entry IP change → exactly one SetPeerRemote + one rehandshake; (3) unchanged active IP → zero calls; (4) idx re-maps when a spec's expansion grows/shrinks; (5) single-IP-literal peer constructs no controller; (6) a hostname spec re-resolving onto the SAME AddrPort as another spec's literal standby leaves the active pointer on its own spec's entry (no spurious re-map, zero SetPeerRemote) and a subsequent failover advance still walks the flattened order correctly.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. - constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. content.text: Now let me explore the current failover.go implementation and related code.

content.content:
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	
8		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
14	)
15	
16	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
17	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
18	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
19	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
20	//
21	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
22	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
23	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
24	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
25	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
26	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
27	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
28	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
29	
30	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
31	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
32	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
33	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
34	type hubHealth interface {
35		State() telemetry.PathState
36	}
37	
38	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
39	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
40	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
41	type peerRemote interface {
42		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
43	}
44	
45	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
46	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
47	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
48	type rehandshake func()
49	
50	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
51	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
52	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
53	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
54	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
55	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
56	// advances again.
57	//
58	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
59	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
60	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
61	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
62	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
63	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
64	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
65	// bounded retry, not a storm.
66	//
67	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
68	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
69	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
70	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
71	// endpoint form, per T54).
72	type hubFailover struct {
73		// endpoints is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint list (config.Peer.Endpoints):
74		// index 0 = active/primary at boot, the rest ordered standbys. Immutable after
75		// construction. IP:port only — no DNS resolution (the T54 constraint).
76		endpoints []netip.AddrPort
77		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
78		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
79		health []hubHealth
80	
81		remote      peerRemote
82		rehandshake rehandshake
83		clock       telemetry.Clock
84		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
85		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
86		settle time.Duration
87		log    log.Logger
88	
89		mu sync.Mutex
90		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint index (0 at boot). Guarded by mu.
91		idx int
92		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
93		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
94		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
95		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
96		lastSwitch time.Time
97	}
98	
99	// newHubFailover builds the controller over the ordered endpoint list and the per-path
100	// liveness set. It is a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the
101	// injected collaborators) so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock,
102	// fake health, and fake remote/rehandshake.
103	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
104		return &hubFailover{
105			endpoints:   endpoints,
106			health:      health,
107			remote:      remote,
108			rehandshake: rh,
109			clock:       clock,
110			settle:      settle,
111			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
112			log:         lg,
113		}
114	}
115	
116	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
117	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
118	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
119	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
120		if len(h.health) == 0 {
121			return false
122		}
123		for _, hp := range h.health {
124			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
125				return false
126			}
127		}
128		return true
129	}
130	
131	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
132	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
133	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
134	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
135	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
136	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
137		h.mu.Lock()
138		defer h.mu.Unlock()
139	
140		// GUARD: a single-endpoint (or empty) list takes NO failover action — no advance, no
141		// remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is byte-for-byte
142		// the pre-T57 behaviour.
143		if len(h.endpoints) < 2 {
144			return
145		}
146		if !h.allDownLocked() {
147			return
148		}
149		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
150		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
151		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
152		now := h.clock.Now()
153		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
154			return
155		}
156	
157		prev := h.idx
158		h.idx = (h.idx + 1) % len(h.endpoints)
159		next := h.endpoints[h.idx]
160		h.lastSwitch = now
161	
162		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
163		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
164		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
165		if h.rehandshake != nil {
166			h.rehandshake()
167		}
168		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
169			"from_index", prev, "to_index", h.idx, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", len(h.endpoints))
170	}
171	
172	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
173	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
174	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
175	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
176	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
177	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
178	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
179	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
180	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
181	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
182		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
183		return func() {
184			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
185			if peer == nil {
186				return
187			}
188			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
189			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
190			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
191			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
192			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
193		}
194	}
195	
196	// startHubFailover builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor for cfg's
197	// concentrator peer, or returns a NO-OP stopper when hub failover does not apply: the
198	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without
199	// the probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer carrying an ordered
200	// (>= 2) endpoint list (a single-concentrator deployment, which the GUARD keeps
201	// behaviour-identical to pre-T57). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE concentrator, so
202	// the whole per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that concentrator (hub
203	// loss = every one DOWN); the controller drives the first peer carrying an ordered
204	// endpoint list. The returned stopper is what device.Close invokes to halt the loop.
205	func startHubFailover(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, lg log.Logger) func() {
206		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
207			return func() {}
208		}
209		for _, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
210			if len(peer.Endpoints) < 2 {
211				continue
212			}
213			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
214			for i, pr := range probers {
215				health[i] = pr
216			}
217			ctrl := newHubFailover(
218				peer.Endpoints, health, mp,
219				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
220				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
221				lg.Component("hubfailover"),
222			)
223			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep)
224			// and the settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens
225			// detection latency without churning the remote.
226			return ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
227		}
228		return func() {}
229	}
230	
231	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
232	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
233	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
234	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
235	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
236	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a single-endpoint list (nothing to fail over to) or a
237	// non-positive interval.
238	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
239		if len(h.endpoints) < 2 || interval <= 0 {
240			return func() {}
241		}
242		done := make(chan struct{})
243		go func() {
244			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
245			defer ticker.Stop()
246			for {
247				select {
248				case <-done:
249					return
250				case <-ticker.C:
251					h.check()
252				}
253			}
254		}()
255		var once sync.Once
256		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
257	}

470		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
471		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
472		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
473		// active one are down. A hostname entry (Q35) contributes NO element here at
474		// load time — resolution happens at runtime, not at config load (Q30) — so
475		// Endpoints only ever holds the literal-address entries, in order.
476		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
477		// DNS is the explicit per-peer opt-in (Q29) for hostname endpoint entries: a
478		// hostname entry in endpoint/endpoints without dns = true is a config load
479		// error naming this flag. Default-off, so an existing IP-literal config never
480		// takes a new code path. Edge-only; a concentrator declaring dns = true is a
481		// config error, mirroring the endpoints-not-meaningful-for-concentrator rule.
482		DNS bool `toml:"dns"`
483		// EndpointSpecs is the ordered, typed per-entry parse of the endpoint/
484		// endpoints entries (Q35): each entry is either a literal address:port
485		// (IsName=false, Addr set — parsed with EXACTLY today's netip.ParseAddrPort
486		// path) or, when DNS opts in, a hostname:port (IsName=true, Host/Port set).
487		// Populated by resolveEndpoints in normalize(); order matches the TOML list.
488		EndpointSpecs []EndpointSpec `toml:"-"`
489		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
490		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
491		// PSK is this peer's per-peer override of the outer-control PSK (G4
492		// multi-peer concentrator groundwork). With a single configured peer it
493		// must be left UNSET: the top-level Config.PSK remains the single-peer
494		// default, so a legacy single-peer config carrying only the top-level
495		// `psk` parses and behaves byte-identically to before this field existed.
496		// With more than one peer, validate requires it to be present and
497		// pairwise-distinct across peers (T81, Q21) — the top-level psk alone
498		// cannot discriminate which peer authenticated an inbound frame, and equal
499		// per-peer psks would defeat that authenticated demux. No datapath code
500		// path consumes PSK yet; it is parsed, validated, and exposed only.
501		PSK Key `toml:"psk"`
502		// Name is this peer's human-readable identifier (G4 multi-peer
503		// concentrator groundwork), analogous to Path.Name. Unused and optional
504		// with a single peer; required and must be unique across peers when more
505		// than one is configured (T81, Q21). Not yet consumed by any datapath code
506		// path.
507		Name string `toml:"name"`
508	}
509	
510	// EndpointSpec is one parsed peer endpoint entry (Q35). A literal address:port
511	// entry parses via netip.ParseAddrPort and sets Addr (IsName=false); a
512	// hostname:port entry (accepted only when the owning Peer opts in with
513	// dns = true) sets Host/Port and IsName=true instead — no resolution happens at
514	// config load (Q30 defers it to runtime).
515	type EndpointSpec struct {
516		// Host is the hostname, set only when IsName is true.
517		Host string
518		// Port is the port for a hostname entry, set only when IsName is true.
519		Port uint16
520		// Addr is the parsed literal address:port, set only when IsName is false.
521		Addr netip.AddrPort
522		// IsName reports whether this entry is a hostname (true) or an IP literal
523		// (false).
524		IsName bool
525	}
526	
527	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
528	// Endpoints list (Q18) and the ordered, typed EndpointSpecs list (Q35): the
529	// legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered EndpointsRaw list are
530	// mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized
531	// here to a ONE-ELEMENT list — so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
532	// single-defaultRemote config surface. Each entry is tried FIRST as a literal
533	// netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint
534	// requires of the UAPI endpoint string at runtime — an all-literal config
535	// takes EXACTLY this path, unchanged, with the same errors and the same
536	// duplicate detection as before T67 (Q29). Only when that parse fails, AND the
537	// host portion is not itself a malformed IP literal, is the entry split as
538	// host:port and treated as a hostname, gated behind the peer's explicit DNS
539	// opt-in (Q29); a hostname entry without the opt-in is a config error naming
540	// the flag. No resolution happens here (Q30 defers it to runtime). Duplicates
541	// are rejected within each of the two namespaces: a literal duplicating a
542	// literal, or a hostname:port duplicating another hostname:port.
543	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
544		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
545			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
546		}
547		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
548		if p.Endpoint != "" {
549			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
550		}
551		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
552		seenNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(raw))
553		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
554		specs := make([]EndpointSpec, 0, len(raw))
555		for _, s := range raw {
556			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
557			if err == nil {
558				if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
559					return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
560				}
561				seen[ap] = struct{}{}
562				endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
563				specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Addr: ap})
564				continue
565			}
566			host, portStr, splitErr := net.SplitHostPort(s)
567			if splitErr != nil {
568				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
569			}
570			// The host portion parses as an IP: this is a malformed IP-literal entry
571			// (e.g. a bad port), not a hostname — report the ORIGINAL ParseAddrPort
572			// error rather than diverting it into the hostname path, so every
573			// IP-shaped entry keeps today's exact error.
574			if _, ipErr := netip.ParseAddr(host); ipErr == nil {
575				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
576			}
577			if !p.DNS {
578				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: hostname endpoints require the peer's dns = true opt-in flag", s)
579			}
580			port, portErr := strconv.ParseUint(portStr, 10, 16)
581			if portErr != nil || port == 0 {
582				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: invalid port %q", s, portStr)
583			}
584			if hostErr := validateHostname(host); hostErr != nil {
585				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, hostErr)
586			}
587			nameKey := host + ":" + portStr
588			if _, dup := seenNames[nameKey]; dup {
589				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
590			}
591			seenNames[nameKey] = struct{}{}
592			specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Host: host, Port: uint16(port), IsName: true})
593		}
594		p.Endpoints = endpoints
595		p.EndpointSpecs = specs
596		return nil
597	}
598	
599	// validateHostname reports whether host is a syntactically valid DNS hostname

content.content:
225	  WG session is untouched. This is the common case.
226	- **Hub failover** (this section): the *concentrator itself* is unreachable —
227	  **every** path's liveness to the active concentrator endpoint is DOWN
228	  simultaneously (HUB LOSS). No surviving uplink can reach it, so switching
229	  uplinks cannot help; the edge must move to a *standby concentrator*.
230	
231	An edge peer carries an **ordered** concentrator endpoint list
232	(`config.Peer.Endpoints`, Q18/T54): index 0 is the active/primary hub, the rest
233	are ordered standbys. Endpoints are **IP:port only — no DNS resolution** (the T54
234	constraint). All hubs in the set share the peer's **single WireGuard static key**,
235	so the same peer identity re-handshakes against whichever hub is active.
236	
237	The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
238	`dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):
239	
240	1. **Detect** hub loss off the **existing per-path liveness plane** — the same
241	   `telemetry.Prober` `State()` the schedulers select on — as *every* path
242	   reporting `StateDown`. No second detector.
243	2. **Advance** to the next endpoint in the ordered list and **repoint every
244	   path's remote** at it via `bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote` (a uniform override —
245	   a hub switch retargets the whole bond; it supersedes any per-path `dest_addr`).
246	   This changes only the per-path fan-out *beneath* the engine's single virtual
247	   endpoint — **invariant A1 holds** (the engine still sees one peer, no
248	   per-packet endpoint churn).
249	3. **Re-handshake**: expire the peer's current keypairs (a **fresh** session —
250	   **no hub-to-hub state handoff**) and send a fresh handshake initiation toward
251	   the just-repointed standby. This is the only engine-*peer* coupling the
252	   failover path takes; it lives in `internal/device` next to the rest of the
253	   engine wiring (the `conn`-seam isolation of `bind.go` is unaffected).
254	4. **Re-baseline the receive resequencer**: the standby is a *separate process*
255	   whose outer sequence restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
256	   hub's high-rate stream advanced the shared `reseq.Resequencer` to. Its first
257	   frame (the WG handshake *response*) would otherwise land in the resequencer's
258	   *suspect* branch and be dropped, because the unauthenticated-DATA resync guard
259	   needs several corroborating low seqs and a freshly re-handshaking standby emits
260	   only ~one DATA frame per `RekeyTimeout` — so corroboration falls outside the
261	   failover window and the tunnel never re-establishes. `SetPeerRemote` therefore
262	   calls `Resequencer.Rebaseline`: because a hub switch is a **trusted control
263	   event** (not a forgeable wire frame), the release point is re-anchored to the
264	   standby's *first* frame immediately, discarding the dead hub's buffered frames
265	   while leaving already-delivered frames untouched.
266	5. **Re-arm** against the new endpoint: probes now flow to it, so if it too is
267	   fully down the controller advances again.
268	
269	**Settle dwell.** After a switch (and at boot for endpoint 0) the newly-selected
270	endpoint gets a fixed dwell (`hubFailoverSettle`, 3 s) to prove itself LIVE before
271	another advance is allowed. It comfortably exceeds the liveness UP-recovery latency
272	(~3 echoes × 200 ms ≈ 600 ms once probes reach a reachable hub), so a still-DOWN
273	reading caused merely by echoes not having returned yet cannot skip past a healthy
274	standby, and it bounds the re-advance cadence (one switch / one handshake per dwell)
275	while a whole hub fleet is down.
276	
277	**End-of-list policy: WRAP** (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last
278	standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
279	endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
280	recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
281	stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
282	endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
283	not a storm.
284	
285	**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
286	action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
287	deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
288	one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
289	this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
290	+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
291	realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).
292	
293	### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`

content.text:
Task complete. Summary of what I implemented.

`internal/device/failover.go` — replaced `hubFailover.endpoints []netip.AddrPort` with `specs []failoverSpec`, where each `failoverSpec` carries its `config.EndpointSpec` and its current expansion `[]netip.AddrPort` (literal → fixed single entry; hostname → latest resolved set, empty until the resolver, which this task does not wire). The flattened concatenation in spec order is the failover order.

Key design points:
- Active endpoint tracked by spec-scoped identity `(activeSpec int, activeAddr netip.AddrPort)` — never a bare flattened index nor a bare value. `idx` is retained as a derived cache (re-mapped after every mutation) so the existing tests that read `h.idx` remain unchanged.
- `flatIndexLocked(specIdx, addr)` matches on BOTH owning spec and AddrPort, so a duplicate AddrPort from a different spec is skipped (the R70 correctness point).
- `updateResolution(specIdx, addrs)` under `h.mu`: standby-only change never touches the bond; active-spec change repoints via exactly one `SetPeerRemote` + rehandshake only when the active AddrPort is gone (D32 no-op suppression when it survives).
- `check()` keeps identical semantics but advances over the flattened list, re-deriving the active position each step.
- `startHubFailover` now gates via `peerNeedsHubFailover` (any hostname spec OR ≥2 flattened literals); single-IP-literal peers get no controller. The loop guard uses `canFailoverLocked`.

`internal/device/failover_test.go` — added six acceptance cases (standby swap → zero repoint; active-IP change → one SetPeerRemote + one rehandshake; unchanged active IP → zero; idx re-map on grow/shrink; `peerNeedsHubFailover` construction gate; R70 cross-spec duplicate). All five pre-existing tests are byte-for-byte unchanged.

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T70 in worktree implement/T70
- **Achieved:** pass; resultCommit 4bd6e683687a7b28702eddab799f2f7b2b6fa5d2; `go test ./internal/device/... -race` green and full `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` green
- **Discovered:** the existing tests read `h.idx` directly, so I kept `idx` as a derived flattened-index cache alongside the authoritative `(specIdx, AddrPort)` identity, and kept `newHubFailover([]netip.AddrPort, …)` as a literal-list convenience constructor so those tests stay unchanged; the new spec-keyed path uses `newHubFailoverFromSpecs`. Boot-time active pointer initializes to the first flattened entry, which correctly falls through empty (unresolved-hostname) specs to the first literal, matching the UAPI `Endpoints[0]` seed.
- **Issues:** none. Resolver wiring is intentionally out of scope (deferred to a later G5 task); design.md needs no change since observable behavior for the currently-wired (all-literal) path is byte-for-byte pre-G5.
